visualGoal: Make a budget-minded newcomer feel the cheapest credible marine rig is free-plus-one-throttle, and that a wheel is the LAST dollar to spend. Real product proof for the DIY boards, button box, VR and free sim; three budget-critical items (Saitek quadrant, G29, BU0836A) are text-only until real photos exist. No procedural art.

realProductPhotosNeeded.product: Logitech G Saitek Pro Flight Throttle Quadrant (as engine telegraph)
realProductPhotosNeeded.notes: THE budget MVP of the whole article and currently text-only because no verified image. Highest-priority photo to source — it is the single most-recommended sub-$100 buy. Needs a real product photo before it can appear as a gearpick/shotgrid or before certification.

realProductPhotosNeeded.product: Leo Bodnar BU0836A USB Joystick Interface (solder version)
realProductPhotosNeeded.notes: The exact board the Bridge Command DIY guide specifies and the cheapest real DIY route (~$42). Text-only in body; the verified BU0836X image is used as the DIY visual stand-in instead. Source a real BU0836A photo to make the cheapest option a body visual.

realProductPhotosNeeded.product: Logitech G G29 Driving Force (as helm)
realProductPhotosNeeded.notes: The 'stretch buy' wheel, deliberately framed as over-budget and last-priority. Text-only in body. Low visual priority since the article's thesis is to NOT buy a wheel first; a photo is optional, not a certification blocker on its own.

editorialCompositePrompts.prompt: Realistic budget marine sim corner on a modest desk at cool evening light: a laptop or older PC running a ship-handling sim, a plain third-party gamepad resting on the desk, a small breadboard-and-potentiometer DIY throttle in progress with a loop of string around a pot shaft for friction, coiled USB cables, an open space left clear for a real DIY-board product overlay, curator Gus Calder leaning in pointing at the string-friction trick, no fake branded hardware, no fake UI, 16:9 editorial cover.
editorialCompositePrompts.negativePrompt: fake logos, fake boat wheel, mythical marine helm console, warped controls, impossible ergonomics, tiny unreadable text, procedural gradient art

infographicPrompts.title: The under-$200 marine sim buy order
content: ~$25 Arduino + one 10k pot = first real axis
~$60 BU0836 + three pots = analog helm + twin throttles
infographicPrompts.style: IgnitionSim command-center numbered ladder, large readable text, marine teal palette, real product photos as rung markers for Codex overlay, arrow emphasizing 'wheel is last, not first'

infographicPrompts.title: The gate that ruins a budget: analog vs keyboard-only
content: Keyboard-only title = wheel becomes binary left/right, throttle axis dead
Fix = free glue software (vJoy / JoyToKey / Xpadder)
Rule: cheapest gear the game reads > priciest rig it ignores
infographicPrompts.style: Two-path decision diagram, marine palette, big readable labels, free-tool badges, no product photos required

curatorScenePrompts.prompt: Realistic adult marine-sim curator Gus Calder in a practical home workshop, one hand holding a small DIY throttle lever with string wrapped around the potentiometer shaft for friction, calm hands-on maker energy, product-overlay space beside him on the bench, practical not fantasy, 4:5 portrait.

screenshotsOrGameMediaNeeded.notes: A screenshot of the free sim running would reinforce the '$0 tier is real, not a compromise' point. Must be sourced from the project's own media/screenshots and credited, not fabricated.

openQuestions: Source real product photos for the Saitek Pro Flight Throttle Quadrant (budget MVP) and the BU0836A (cheapest DIY board) — the two highest-value text-only items?
Confirm MarineVerse pricing model: is it now $29.99 one-time, still free-with-Sailing-Pass, or both? Body currently says $29.99 one-time.
Confirm current USD landed cost (incl. UK shipping) for BU0836A (~$42) and BBI-32 (~$53) before quoting hard numbers.
Verify whether Sailaway still offers a live free demo in 2026 (body flags it as 'check current').
